title: Designing a Faith Streaming App That Feels Premium Without Defaulting to Dark Mode
subtitle: Redesigning Bibel TV's mobile, web, and TV apps to defend against a global competitor — with research, infrastructure, and agentic engineering
team: 1 designer (me) + 1 direct report (potential) + cross-functional partners, Berlin

hero_image.alt: Bibel TV app redesign: Mediathek section with warm light mode aesthetic and dynamic color theming

sections.headline: Designing a Faith Streaming App That Feels Premium Without Defaulting to Dark Mode
sections.subtext: Pray.com was launching in Germany — a premium faith app from ex-Netflix/Spotify engineers. Bibel TV has more content and 300+ years of archive, but a weaker UX. The redesign had to defend our market position by building a premium experience that feels warm, not Hollywood. That meant making light mode design genuinely work where the industry defaults to dark.

images.alt: Before and after: Bibel TV app redesign comparison
images.caption: Before: dated patterns, no brand alignment. After: streaming-quality design with warm identity.

sections.quote: Premium streaming apps default to dark mode. It's the industry standard. But our users want light. The question wasn't which trend to follow — it was how to make premium work the way our users needed it to.
sections.context: The strategic design tension at the heart of the project

sections.headline: The Business Case
sections.content:
Bibel TV is the largest faith media platform in Europe with 300+ years of broadcast archive. But our app was lagging:

- **Mediocre UI patterns** — not aligned with modern streaming apps
- **No brand connection** — broadcast rebranded in 2024, app still looked 2020
- **Limited content discovery** — users couldn't find what they wanted
- **No habit formation** — no behavioral design for engagement or retention

The competitive threat was Pray.com: well-funded, built by ex-Netflix/Spotify engineers, launching in Germany with a beautiful dark-mode-first app. They were built for a global market. Bibel TV has German market knowledge and cultural legitimacy. That's our moat.

The design work had to make that moat visible. Users needed to feel that Bibel TV understood them — and that the product quality matched the content depth.

sections.headline: Three Integrated Initiatives
sections.content:
A single app redesign would have been surface-level. We built three initiatives that reinforced each other:

**1. App Redesign (Mediathek-first)**
New visual language: lighter, warmer, premium-feeling. Aligned with the 2024 broadcast rebrand. Streaming-native patterns (discovery, pagination, search, watch-next). Three core sections: Mediathek (VOD), Bible Reading, Reading Plans. Behavioral design for retention.

**2. Design System as API**
Not a Figma library — a live token service. GitHub + Style Dictionary + n8n sync. Platforms: Android (live), iOS (planned), web (planned), Figma (synced). One source of truth, automatic sync.

**3. OKLCH Color Extraction API**
Custom K-means clustering in perceptual color space. Extracts themes from content artwork for both light and dark modes. WCAG-compliant. Live config — no code deploys needed.

sections.headline: The Process
sections.content:
The work happened in three phases over approximately 9 months:

**Phase 1: Research (Weeks 1–2)**
Competitive analysis — Netflix, Spotify, Apple TV+, YouTube, Pray.com, Hallow. How do global streaming apps handle discovery, personalization, engagement? Internal stakeholder interviews — PM, engineering, content, leadership. Brand alignment study — how does the broadcast identity translate to digital?

**Phase 2: Direction & Prototyping (Weeks 3–6)**
We converged on aesthetic direction: light, warm, premium. Built a web prototype first for rapid iteration. But when we moved to Android, gradient rendering quality raised concerns. We pivoted to Android prototyping — and the design direction worked beautifully. The lesson: prototype on the target platform early. Web-first assumptions don't always hold.

**Phase 3: Validation (Weeks 7–9)**
Internal round: staff/team testing on UI clarity, navigation, color contrast. External round: user surveys (quantitative) and 10+ follow-up conversations (qualitative). Key findings: 87% preferred light mode. 4.2/5 premium feel rating. No critical UX issues flagged. The design direction held.

sections.headline: Key Design Decisions
sections.content:
**Light mode as the primary design direction** — The industry standard for premium streaming is dark mode. We chose light because our users associate darkness with impersonality. This required making OKLCH color extraction work for light mode — which the industry hasn't solved. It was the harder path but the right one for this brand.

**App redesign before infrastructure, infrastructure before everything else** — We designed the app first to validate direction. But the token API and color extraction had to run in parallel. Without infrastructure, the redesign would be static. With it, the redesign becomes dynamic and self-updating.

**Agentic engineering as design method** — I used Claude Code and Cursor to prototype the web and Android experiences with real APIs and real data. The prototype code became the design artifact, not throwaway mockups. Engineering consumed the prototype directly — no Figma handoff, no specs, no ambiguity. This is how I'm changing the design-to-code pipeline for the whole team.

sections.headline: The Android Pivot
sections.content:
A moment worth calling out: we built a web prototype first because it's fast. The gradients looked beautiful. But when we prototyped Android, we discovered gradient rendering quality was uncertain — banding potential, performance questions on lower-end devices.

Instead of forcing the design direction to accommodate web assumptions, we pivoted and built a full Android prototype. It worked *better* than the web version. The design direction was sound — the problem was our prototyping approach, not our design.

This is a lesson I carry into every multi-platform project: prototype on the target platform early, or your assumptions will be wrong.

steps.label: Design to Code (Not Figma to Handoff)
steps.description: I designed new experiences as functional web and Android prototypes using Claude Code and Cursor. Real APIs, real content data. Not static mockups.

steps.label: Prototype as Design Artifact
steps.description: The prototype code became the design spec — component structure, API calls, interaction patterns. Engineering consumed it directly.

steps.label: Parallel Iteration
steps.description: Design and engineering worked in parallel, not sequentially. I could iterate quickly on engineer feedback because the design was already in code.

steps.label: Evangelizing the Method
steps.description: Active evangelism with the broader team — trying to shift how design and engineering collaborate from sequential handoff to parallel development.

metrics.label: User Preference
metrics.value: 87% prefer light mode, 4.2/5 premium feel rating — validated across surveys and conversations

metrics.label: Design System
metrics.value: Token API live, Android consuming tokens, Figma synced via n8n. Single source of truth.

metrics.label: Color Extraction
metrics.value: OKLCH API running — all palettes WCAG AA compliant, no manual intervention needed

metrics.label: Readiness
metrics.value: Design finalized. Moving to development handover. Engineering team has functional prototype code.

sections.headline: What I Learned
sections.content:
The biggest learning: owning design end-to-end means owning the infrastructure. Not just the Figma file — the token API, the color extraction logic, the automation workflows. These are design decisions embedded in code. When you delegate them, you delegate the quality of the final product.

The second learning: prototyping in code with agentic tools is not a compromise — it's a step up. I used Claude Code to explore faster, iterate more broadly, and produce design artifacts that engineering could consume directly. The traditional Figma-to-handoff model is slower and introduces more ambiguity.

The third: the Android pivot taught me to prototype on the target platform early. Web-first is fast. Target-platform-first is honest.

sections.headline: Why This Matters for Your Team
sections.content: If you're leading design across multiple platforms without a large team, you can't afford the luxury of sequential workflows. I designed the entire Bibel TV app experience — across mobile, web, and TV — as a single head of product design. The secret was treating infrastructure as design work and code as a design medium. That's not a technical skill — it's a leadership decision. When you collapse the design-to-code pipeline, you ship better products with fewer people.

executive.summary: Led a full app redesign (mobile, web, TV) with custom color extraction API, deployed token system, and agentic engineering. 87% light mode user preference, 4.2/5 premium rating. Ready for development handover.
executive.angle: Multi-platform product leadership with technical depth — from competitive strategy through infrastructure

blog.summary: A global competitor was launching with a premium dark-mode app. We chose light mode because our users wanted warmth, not Hollywood. Here's how we made it work.
blog.angle: Design leadership for smaller teams: when to own the infrastructure and when to delegate

meta.source: Bibel TV project interview 2026-05-06 + comprehensive project case study
